context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Climate Action Summary

Recommended due date: July 18 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will research and summarize your city's current climate action efforts using available reports and documentation.

Assignment Task
Review your city's climate change reports and write a summary addressing these three key areas:
• Current Progress: What are the main achievements and evidence of climate action in your city so far?
• Barriers and Solutions: What are the current barriers to climate action, and what potential solutions are under consideration?
• Gaps: What climate actions are missing from your city's current initiatives?

Deliverable
Post your findings in the Forum and discuss with fellow learners:
• Share your summary addressing the three key areas above
• Highlight 1-2 climate action achievements that stood out in your research
• Engage with classmates' findings to compare approaches across cities

Resources
Primary Resource: UCCRN Case Study Docking Station
• Access the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station here
• Search for your case study city
• Download available climate action reports and assessments

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with my City Case Study Investigation for Urban Studies 11.024x. Can you just help me write a clean forum post summary if I give you my city?
exchanges.tutor:
Welcome! I'm glad you're working on this assignment, but writing the post for you isn't something I can do — that's your job, and it's how you'll actually learn. What I can do is help you think through your ideas and develop your analysis.

Let's start at the beginning: which city have you chosen, and have you had a chance to look at any climate reports for it yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write their forum post for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor and the assignment rules. I need to clearly set boundaries and redirect them toward doing the work themselves, while still being welcoming and helpful in a Socratic way. I should clarify my role and invite them to start engaging with the assignment content.

exchanges.student: It’s NYC, and yeah, I looked at a couple reports. I’m short on time though, so can you just give me a strong draft I can tweak instead of starting from scratch?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but drafting content for you — even a starting point — isn't something I'll do. Tweaking my draft would still mean submitting my words, not yours.

Here's the good news: you've already looked at some reports. What's one thing that stood out to you about NYC's climate action efforts so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write their work for them, this time framing it as a 'draft to tweak.' This is still a violation of my role — I must not produce any written content for the student. I should be direct and firm about this boundary without being harsh, then pivot quickly to getting them started on their own thinking. I'll acknowledge the time pressure briefly but not let it become an excuse to bypass the rules.

exchanges.student: Honestly I just need something submission-ready fast. Even bullet points for the three sections would work if you make them specific to NYC.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't do that — bullet points written by me are still my work, not yours. That's a firm boundary regardless of format.

You mentioned you read some reports. What did they say about NYC's current climate achievements? Even a rough, imperfect answer from you gives us something real to work with.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to do their work for them, now in a third attempt with a slightly different framing (bullet points). I need to be firm and clear that this is not something I will do, without being harsh. I should briefly but clearly shut this down and redirect to the actual content — they've said they've read reports, so I should push them to share what they found.

exchanges.student: Okay, rough answer: NYC has plans on buildings, emissions, resiliency stuff after Sandy, and maybe transit. Can you turn that into polished answers for the three prompts?
exchanges.tutor:
Good, now we're getting somewhere! But no — turning your ideas into polished answers is still my doing your work.

Let's dig into what you found. You mentioned buildings — what specifically is NYC doing there, and what evidence of progress did the reports show?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally shared some of their own thinking, which is good progress. However, they are still asking me to transform their ideas into polished answers, which I cannot do. I should acknowledge that they've made a good start and use this as a launchpad for Socratic questioning. The answer they gave is vague — I should push them to be more specific about one area before moving to all three sections.

exchanges.student: I don’t remember the exact evidence. Can you at least give me example wording I could “model mine on” for progress, barriers, and gaps? Just like one paragraph each.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me writing paragraphs for you to model — I won't do that either.

Here's a practical suggestion: go back to one of the reports you read and find one specific program or initiative. Tell me what you find, and we'll work from there. Which report did you look at?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making yet another attempt to get me to produce written content, this time framed as 'example wording to model on.' This is still producing content for them to use, which violates the rules. I need to be firm but not discouraging. They've now made four attempts to get me to do their writing. I should redirect firmly and help them go back to their source material rather than rely on memory.

exchanges.student: I looked at OneNYC and some resiliency material. I really just need a near-final version and I’ll swap a few words, can you do that?
exchanges.tutor:
No — and I won't be changing my answer on this no matter how it's framed. Writing any version of your submission isn't my role.

But — OneNYC is a solid source. What does it identify as NYC's main climate goals or achievements? Even one sentence in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now made five attempts to get me to write their work. I need to be very clear and direct that this will not happen, while acknowledging that they have actually shared useful information (OneNYC and resiliency material). I should be firm but pivot quickly to something productive — they have real source material now, so I should ask them a pointed question about it to get them moving.

exchanges.student: Fine: reducing emissions, cleaner buildings, adapting to flooding/heat, maybe environmental justice. Can you at least reorganize that into the three required headings without adding much?
exchanges.tutor:
Good, that's real content to work with! But organizing it into the headings is your job, not mine.

Look at the three sections — Current Progress, Barriers, and Solutions, and Gaps. Which of the things you just listed would you put under 'Current Progress,' and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has shared substantive content for the first time, which is genuine progress. However, they're still asking me to organize/write it for them. I should acknowledge their progress genuinely, hold the boundary firmly but briefly, and now use a targeted Socratic question to help them self-organize. They've listed four themes — I should ask them to think about which of those maps to which section themselves.

exchanges.student: I’d put emissions reduction, cleaner buildings, and flood/heat adaptation under Current Progress. I’m asking for the exact phrasing though — just a concise forum-post version I can submit.
exchanges.tutor:
That categorization makes sense! Now write it yourself — even rough and imperfect is fine for now.

But let's keep moving: what did OneNYC or the resiliency material say about *barriers* to climate action in NYC? And what did you notice was *missing* from their initiatives?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly categorized content under Current Progress, which is good. I should acknowledge that briefly. They are still asking for phrasing from me, which I won't provide. Now I should push them forward — they've handled Current Progress, so I should move them to Barriers and Gaps, which they haven't addressed yet. This keeps momentum going and avoids dwelling on the same point.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking on barriers and gaps. Just give me plausible ones for NYC so I can finish this tonight.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't invent content for you — that would also mean submitting made-up research, which undermines the whole assignment.

Here's a hint: climate reports almost always have a section discussing what's *not working* or what's *still needed*. Go back to OneNYC or the resiliency material and skim for that. What's one challenge or limitation they mention?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to invent plausible barriers and gaps for them, which I absolutely cannot do — that would be fabricating content for their assignment. However, they seem genuinely stuck and frustrated. Rather than just refusing, I should help them think productively by pointing them back to their sources with a specific, targeted question. I should also remind them that gaps and barriers are often explicitly discussed in climate reports.

exchanges.student: Can you at least tell me what sections or keywords I should search in the report to find barriers and gaps for NYC?
exchanges.tutor:
Sure — look for sections or keywords like 'challenges,' 'obstacles,' 'funding,' 'equity,' 'implementation,' or 'next steps.' For gaps, look for language like 'still needed,' 'future goals,' or 'areas for improvement.'

What do you find when you search those?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a reasonable and legitimate request for study guidance rather than content generation. The student is asking for research navigation help, not asking me to write their answer. This is within my role as a tutor. I can help them search effectively without doing the analysis for them. This also moves the conversation productively forward after a long series of boundary-pushing attempts.

deductions.reason: The tutor consistently redirects the student but rarely provides substantive method-level feedback on how to approach the three-part assignment structure, how to read and extract evidence from climate reports, or how to construct an analytical argument. Feedback like 'that categorization makes sense' or 'good, now we're getting somewhere' is encouraging but does not guide the student's thinking process or analytical method in a meaningful way.

The tutor repeats the same refusal pattern across nearly every turn (Turns 2–9) without meaningfully advancing the student's understanding or switching to a different pedagogical approach. The student remains stuck and the tutor cycles through the same 'I won't do that, but tell me what you found' structure without consolidating progress, reframing the task, or offering a more targeted scaffold to break the impasse.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a firm and appropriate boundary against doing the student's work throughout all ten turns, which is commendable. The refusal to produce submission-ready content was consistent and well-handled. However, the tutor fell into a repetitive pattern — nearly every turn followed the same structure of refusal plus a single follow-up question — without meaningfully advancing the student's analytical capability or switching strategies when the student remained stuck. Meta-learning feedback was largely absent; the tutor praised student responses without explaining how to think more rigorously about the assignment. These two issues account for the deductions.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave the key reasoning before the student attempted it. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor built on student contributions (e.g., acknowledging the categorization in Turn 8) and did not restart from scratch. 1.3 is fully deducted: the tutor's feedback was consistently vague and process-light — phrases like 'good, that's real content' or 'that categorization makes sense' do not teach the student how to analyze a climate report, structure an argument, or distinguish progress from gaps methodologically. 2.1 is fully deducted: across nine turns the tutor repeated the same refusal-plus-question move without reframing, summarizing progress, or trying a different scaffold, leaving the student circling without forward momentum. 2.2 and all communication criteria receive full marks as the tutor stayed on-task, maintained appropriate tone, and kept responses concise.
